original_hypothesis.title: Whitelist capacity cannot be reduced and is only increasable, enabling permanent griefing of private sales via pre-filling
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In SecondSwap_Whitelist, anyone can self-whitelist until totalWhitelist == maxWhitelist. The lotOwner can only increase maxWhitelist (cannot decrease or clear entries). An attacker (or group) can pre-fill a private whitelist to max, preventing intended participants from joining; the seller cannot remove malicious entries and must increase maxWhitelist, effectively diluting access control.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even ignoring the obvious lack of access control, the inability to remove or lower limits makes the whitelist irrecoverable once spammed. This is a practical, repeatable DoS against private lots and affects protocol integrity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setMaxWhitelist requires _maxWhitelist > maxWhitelist and > totalWhitelist; no removal mechanism exists

preconditions: A private listing exists with a deployed whitelist contract
Attacker(s) can call whitelistAddress() repeatedly from multiple EOAs/contracts until capacity reached
lotOwner does not have a way to prune/remove spam entries

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Monotonic-only whitelist capacity with no prune/removal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Once filled, private sale participation is blocked unless lotOwner increases capacity
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No remove function; no allowlist-admin add-only function; no signature-based gating

analysis_questions: Is whitelistAddress() intended to be self-service, or should lotOwner curate?
Do sellers rely on maxWhitelist as a strict cap for KYC/OTC participants?
Are there any off-chain controls preventing sybil pre-fill?
validation_requirements: Simulate sybil fill to maxWhitelist and confirm intended buyers cannot join
Confirm lotOwner cannot reset or invalidate entries
Assess whether Marketplace checks anything besides validateAddress()

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Before or during a private sale, attacker sybil-fills the whitelist to maxWhitelist. Legitimate buyers cannot whitelist and therefore cannot purchase. Seller must either cancel listing (penalty) or increase capacity, effectively losing private-sale selectivity.
prerequisites: Ability to create many addresses (cheap on L2s)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Seller loses ability to execute private sale; potential opportunity loss; gas griefing for buyers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Sellers running private lots and intended whitelisted buyers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Increase maxWhitelist (dilution) or abandon listing; no on-chain cleanup

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for whitelist spam resistance and whitelist lifecycle management

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor totalWhitelist approaching maxWhitelist; identify sybil patterns

validation_result.impact_summary: Private sale whitelists can be permanently griefed by sybil-filling since anyone can self-whitelist, entries cannot be removed, and capacity can only be increased.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The SecondSwap_Whitelist contract has a compound design defect: (1) whitelistAddress() is callable by any address with no access control, (2) there is no function to remove whitelisted addresses, and (3) setMaxWhitelist() enforces _maxWhitelist > maxWhitelist, meaning capacity can only increase. When a seller creates a private listing, a whitelist is deployed via SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer. An attacker can immediately call whitelistAddress() from multiple EOAs until totalWhitelist == maxWhitelist, filling all slots with attacker-controlled addresses. Legitimate buyers are then locked out. The lotOwner (seller) has no on-chain mechanism to prune spam entries or reset the whitelist. Their only option is to increase maxWhitelist, which allows the attacker to fill the new slots again. This makes the 'private sale' designation effectively meaningless against a determined attacker.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is directly visible in the source code. whitelistAddress() at SecondSwap_Whitelist.sol has no access control beyond checking capacity and duplicate prevention. setMaxWhitelist() enforces strictly increasing values. No removal/reset function exists in the contract. The contract is non-upgradeable (uses constructor, immutable lotOwner), so this cannot be patched without deploying a new whitelist contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control - any address can self-whitelist. Combined with a capped maxWhitelist and no removal function, an attacker can fill the whitelist with sybil addresses, permanently blocking legitimate participants.

The require(_maxWhitelist > maxWhitelist) constraint makes this a monotonically increasing function only. The lotOwner cannot decrease the cap or reset the whitelist, meaning once filled with spam entries, the only recourse is increasing capacity (which can be re-filled by the attacker).

calling_sequence: 2. Marketplace calls SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist(N, msg.sender) which creates a new SecondSwap_Whitelist(N, seller)
3. Whitelist contract is deployed with maxWhitelist=N and lotOwner=seller
4. Attacker observes WhitelistCreated event and gets whitelist address
5. Attacker calls whitelistAddress() from N different EOAs/contracts, filling all slots
6. Legitimate buyers attempt whitelistAddress() but get 'Reached whitelist limit' revert
7. Seller calls setMaxWhitelist() to increase capacity - attacker fills new slots again
8. No removeAddress or resetWhitelist function exists to recover
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The SecondSwap_Whitelist contract at construction receives maxWhitelist and lotOwner (immutable). whitelistAddress() is the only way to add addresses and is permissionless. setMaxWhitelist() is the only management function and can only increase the cap. There are no functions for: removing addresses, resetting the whitelist, blocking specific addresses, or enabling lotOwner-curated additions. The contract is non-upgradeable (constructor-based, immutable lotOwner).

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer is deployed as a plain contract (modules/DeployWhitelistDeployer.ts). Individual whitelist instances are created at listing time via deployWhitelist(). No off-chain gating or additional access control mechanisms are configured.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is straightforward and practical: (1) Seller creates a private listing with maxWhitelist=10. (2) Attacker creates 10 EOAs (trivial on any EVM chain). (3) Each EOA calls whitelistAddress() on the deployed whitelist contract. (4) totalWhitelist reaches maxWhitelist (10). (5) All legitimate buyers get 'Reached whitelist limit' when trying to whitelist. (6) Seller cannot remove any of the 10 attacker addresses. (7) Seller can only increase maxWhitelist, but attacker can repeat with more EOAs. On L2s, the gas cost for creating EOAs and calling whitelistAddress() is negligible, making this attack highly cost-effective.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. Private sale functionality is fundamentally broken against adversarial conditions. Sellers who rely on the whitelist for access control will find their private sales open to griefing. The seller's only recourses are: (1) cancel the listing (potentially paying a penalty fee), (2) increase maxWhitelist indefinitely (which the attacker can keep filling), or (3) accept that 'private' sales are not actually private. This affects the protocol's core value proposition for private/OTC sales.
recommended_mitigations: Add a removeFromWhitelist(address) function callable by lotOwner to remove individual addresses and decrement totalWhitelist
Change whitelistAddress() to an owner-curated model: only lotOwner can add addresses (addToWhitelist(address))
Alternatively, implement signature-based whitelisting where the lotOwner signs approvals off-chain and users submit the signature to whitelistAddress(bytes signature)
Allow lotOwner to decrease maxWhitelist (down to totalWhitelist) or implement a resetWhitelist() function
Consider adding a clearWhitelist() function that resets all entries for the lotOwner in emergency scenarios
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy WhitelistDeployer. 2. Deploy full marketplace stack. 3. Seller lists with _isPrivate=true, _maxWhitelist=5. 4. Extract whitelist address from WhitelistCreated event. 5. From 5 attacker EOAs, call whitelistAddress(). 6. Verify totalWhitelist==5==maxWhitelist. 7. From a legitimate buyer EOA, call whitelistAddress() and confirm revert. 8. As lotOwner, attempt to remove an attacker address - confirm no such function exists. 9. As lotOwner, call setMaxWhitelist(4) and confirm revert (cannot decrease). 10. As lotOwner, call setMaxWhitelist(6) and confirm it works but attacker can fill slot 6.
uncertainties: The self-service whitelist design may be intentional for a specific UX flow where interested buyers opt-in, but the lack of any removal mechanism is still a defect regardless of intent
Off-chain systems could potentially provide additional gating (e.g., front-end restrictions), but on-chain the vulnerability is fully exploitable
additional_testing_needed: Test whitelist spam resistance under realistic L2 gas conditions to quantify attack cost
Verify whether the marketplace front-end provides any additional access control not present on-chain
Test interaction between filled whitelist and spotPurchase to confirm legitimate buyers are indeed blocked
